items.title: 4/26: Sunday Morning
items.body: Hosted by Jane Pauley. Featured: America's adversarial relationship with Cuba; singer-songwriter Kacey Musgraves; Rep. Jim Clyburn; reviving a Welsh soccer town; tree lovers; artist Jenny Saville; and rescuing Venus fly traps.

items.title: Israel and Hezbollah Trade Strikes in Lebanon, as Iran Talks Remain on Hold
items.body: Cease-fires in Lebanon and Iran are on shaky ground, with military attacks flaring and direct talks between Washington and Tehran to end their war stalled.

items.title: Iran's foreign minister returns to Pakistan after U.S. envoys' trip canceled
items.body: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi returned to Islamabad on Sunday, where he is expected to participate in talks focused on ending the war with the U.S., Iranian state media reported.

items.title: Accused shooter was targeting Trump and US officials, authorities say
items.body: President Donald Trump says suspect wrote an anti-Christian declaration and is 'sick guy'.

items.title: Israel’s President, Putting Off Decision on Pardon for Netanyahu, Will Push for Plea Deal
items.body: President Isaac Herzog of Israel has decided not to issue a pardon to Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in his corruption case at this time, and instead will seek mediation, officials say.

items.title: First trial of Assad-era Syrian official starts in Damascus
items.body: Syria begun its first public trial of former Assad-era officials, with ex-security chief Atef Najib appearing in court.

items.title: Transcript: Sam Vinograd and AT Smith on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," April 26, 2026
items.body: The following is the transcript of the interview with CBS News national security contributor Sam Vinograd and CBS News law enforcement analyst AT Smith that aired on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" on April 26, 2026.

items.title: Weijia Jiang on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," April 26, 2026
items.body: The following is the transcript of the interview with White House Correspondents' Association President and senior CBS News correspondent Weijia Jiang that aired on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" on April 26, 2026.

items.title: Transcript: Sir Christian Turner, U.K. ambassador to the U.S., on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," April 26, 2026
items.body: The following is the transcript of the interview with Sir Christian Turner, U.K. ambassador to the U.S., that aired on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" on April 26, 2026.

items.title: Transcript: Chevron CEO Mike Wirth on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," April 26, 2026
items.body: The following is the transcript of the interview with Chevron CEO Mike Wirth that aired on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" on April 26, 2026.

items.title: Iran and U.S. Sink Into Awkward Limbo of ‘No War, No Peace’
items.body: Each side is betting it can last longer than the other, analysts say. But there are risks in a stalemate without a deal.

items.title: Transcript: Rep. Jamie Raskin on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," April 26, 2026
items.body: The following is the transcript of the interview with Rep. Jamie Raskin, Democrat of Maryland, that aired on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" on April 26, 2026.

items.title: Thai police arrest Indonesian wanted for $10m cyberfraud
items.body: The 33-year-old Indonesian taken into custody at luxury resort on island of Phuket after FBI tip-off.

items.title: Iran shifts economic focus to essentials during war uncertainty
items.body: The government is partly reversing a currency decision for basic items and tapping into the country's sovereign fund.

items.title: Abbas loyalists sweep Palestinian elections, including some seats in Gaza
items.body: Voter turnout in Gaza remained low due to the ongoing impact of the genocide.

items.title: Canadian Province of Manitoba Says It Will Ban Social Media, AI For Youth
items.body: The Canadian province of Manitoba plans to prohibit young people from accessing social media and artificial intelligence chatbots, its leader said.

items.title: Italy Decides to Extradite Chinese Man Wanted by US for Hacking
items.body: The Italian government decided to extradite a Chinese man wanted by the US on hacking charges, people familiar with the matter said on Sunday.

items.title: New footage shows moment of deadly bus bombing in Colombia
items.body: Newly released dashcam video shows the moment that a bomb-laden bus exploded in Colombia.

items.title: ‘State of war’: Why Israel has escalated attacks in Gaza
items.body: Israel escalates attacks and expands control in Gaza Strip, sidelining the new US-backed technocratic administration.

items.title: 4/26: Face The Nation
items.body: This week on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," acting Attorney General Todd Blanche and White House Correspondents' Association President and CBS News correspondent Weijia Jiang join after gunshots rang out at the annual dinner. Plus, U.K. Ambassador to the U.K., Sir Christian Turner, joins.

items.title: Will Yamal, Salah and Ekitike miss the World Cup 2026 due to injury?
items.body: From Spanish prodigy Lamine Yamal to Egypt's Mohamed Salah and Brazil's trio, these 10 players could be out of action.

items.title: Apple’s Cook Gives Ternus a Pipeline of 10 Major New Product Categories
items.body: Also: John Ternus talks AI and services with employees.

items.title: Mezcal worm in a bottle DNA test reveals a surprise
items.body: The famous mezcal “worm” has long puzzled scientists, but DNA testing has finally cracked the case. Researchers found that all sampled larvae were actually agave redworm moth caterpillars—not a mix of species as once believed. While the discovery clears up a long-standing mystery, it also raises concerns about sustaina

items.title: How China is responding to the stalling of talks between the U.S. and Iran
items.body: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to Zongyuan Zoe Liu, a senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations, about how China views the current crisis in the Middle East

items.title: Musk Vies to Turn X Into Super App With Banking Tool Near Launch
items.body: More than three years after acquiring Twitter, Elon Musk says he’s nearing his long-stated goal of turning it into an “everything app” with a new financial services tool that he pledged to launch for the public this month.

items.title: U.S. Mint Buys Drug Cartel Gold and Sells It as ‘American’
items.body: As prices for the precious metal soar, the industry’s guardrails have broken down.

items.title: Blood vessels found in T. rex bones are rewriting dinosaur science
items.body: Dinosaur DNA may still be out of reach, but scientists are uncovering something almost as exciting—ancient blood vessels hidden inside fossilized bones. In a massive Tyrannosaurus rex nicknamed Scotty, researchers discovered a network of preserved vessels within a rib that once fractured and began healing 66 million ye

items.title: Trump doubts shooter motivated by Iran war as peace talks on hold
items.body: A shooting incident at the White House Correspondent's Dinner took focus away from the war in Iran, as Iran's foreign minister planned to return to Islamabad, the site of previous peace talks.

items.title: DNA research just rewrote the origin of human species
items.body: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new picture of human origins that challenges the long-held idea of a single ancestral population in Africa. By analyzing genetic data from diverse modern African groups—especially the highly distinct Nama people—and comparing it with fossil evidence, researchers found that early h

items.title: 40 Years After the Meltdown, War Layers Another Disaster on Chernobyl
items.body: Ideas have been floated for how the contaminated zone could bring economic benefits to Ukraine. But for the foreseeable future, it will be an army-controlled security belt.

items.title: China Formalizes Labor Rules for Gig Workers on Online Platforms
items.body: China formalized its first comprehensive policy framework for “new employment groups,” including delivery riders and livestreamers, signaling a shift from ad-hoc regulation to normalization of the platform economy.

items.title: Islamabad Reopens After U.S.-Iran Talks Fail to Materialize
items.body: Officials had locked the city down, anticipating talks between U.S. and Iranian delegations. But they didn’t happen. “What did I close my business for?” one business owner asked.

items.title: King Charles U.S. visit comes at tense moment in transatlantic relationship
items.body: King Charles and Queen Camilla arrive Monday for a four day U.S. state visit. Some hope the royal touch can heal the transatlantic rift that's emerged under Trump.

items.title: What Happened After The New York Times Found a Cartel Mine on a Colombian Military Base
items.body: Officers denied that an illegal, large-scale gold operation was underway within earshot of their posts. But we had seen it with our own eyes.

items.title: Armed groups launch coordinated attacks across Mali
items.body: Witnesses report clashes in the centre and north, in what has been described as the largest jihadist attack in years.

items.title: Fish oil may be hurting your brain, new study finds
items.body: Fish oil has long been praised as brain-boosting, but new research suggests the story may be more complicated. Scientists found that in people with repeated mild head injuries, a key omega-3 fatty acid in fish oil—EPA—may actually interfere with the brain’s ability to repair itself. Instead of helping recovery, it appe

items.title: DeepSeek V4 Delay Shows Shift to China Chips, CCTV Account Says
items.body: DeepSeek’s delayed release of its V4 model points to a strategic shift toward deeper integration with China’s domestic chip ecosystem, according to Yuyuantantian, a social media account affiliated with the government-controlled China Central Television.

items.title: I was in the room with Trump and heard the low thudding sound of gunfire
items.body: The BBC's Gary O'Donoghue describes the moment he and others dived for cover as shots rang out at the venue.

items.title: How Nieves González’s Painting of Lily Allen Made Her Famous
items.body: Nieves González, a 29-year-old painter, once worked in relative obscurity in Andalusia. Her picture of the British singer Lily Allen changed that.

items.title: Aggressive “hulk” lizards are wiping out millions of years of evolution
items.body: For ages, wall lizards coexisted in three distinct color types, each with its own strategy for survival. Now, a powerful green variant is taking over. These dominant “Hulk” lizards are outcompeting the others, causing yellow and orange morphs to vanish. It’s a dramatic reminder that evolution can flip the script much f

items.title: Beijing Warns EU After China Firms Included in Russia Sanctions
items.body: China said it will take necessary measures after the European Union added Chinese entities to its latest Russia sanctions package, warning that all consequences would be borne by the EU.

items.title: Netanyahu orders army to 'vigorously attack' Hezbollah in Lebanon
items.body: The Israeli PM's directive comes as six people were killed by strikes in Lebanon despite a ceasefire being extended by three weeks.

items.title: In a rare interview, a leader of the world's largest right-wing group talks to NPR
items.body: The second-in-command of the RSS, a Hindu nationalist organization in India, rarely speaks to the Western press. Here's what he said about his group's controversial history.

items.title: Trump cancels US envoys' trip to Pakistan for talks on Iran war
items.body: Iran had earlier said there were no plans for a direct meeting with a US delegation led by Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner.

items.title: Militants and separatists launch coordinated attacks across Mali
items.body:
Al-Qaida-linked group JNIM claims responsibility for strikes on airport in capital, Bamako and four other cities

Islamic militants and separatists attacked several locations in Mali’s capital and other cities on Saturday in one of the largest coordinated attacks in the country in recent years.

The al-Qai

items.title: Orbán steps down from Hungarian parliament after landslide defeat
items.body: The outgoing prime minister will not take up his seat after leading his party back into opposition.

items.title: Mexico says US agents killed in crash weren't permitted to operate there
items.body: The two Americans who reportedly worked for the CIA died in a car crash after a Mexican-led operation to destroy a drug lab.

items.title: Rights groups critical as Venezuela prisoner release scheme 'coming to an end'
items.body: More than 500 political prisoners are thought to still be in jail, despite the releases since the amnesty law was brought in.

items.title: Palestinians in West Bank and some in Gaza vote in local elections
items.body: Local elections have been held in the occupied West Bank and in one Gazan city, though Hamas and other groups are not taking part.

items.title: Harvard scientists link gut bacteria to depression through hidden inflammation trigger
items.body: A gut bacterium may be quietly fueling depression through an unexpected chemical twist. Researchers found that when Morganella morganii interacts with a common pollutant, it produces a molecule that triggers inflammation—something strongly linked to depression. This finding helps explain how gut microbes can influence

items.title: Mali reeling after coordinated attacks hit multiple cities
items.body: Gunfire and explosions rocked Mali's capital and other key cities in one of the most significant coordinated attacks in years, as armed groups exploited worsening insecurity in the Sahel region.

items.title: This exotic particle could finally explain why matter has mass
items.body: A major physics experiment has uncovered evidence for a strange new form of matter, where a fleeting particle gets trapped inside a nucleus. This exotic state may reveal how mass is generated, suggesting that particles can weigh less when surrounded by dense nuclear matter. The findings support long-standing theories a

items.title: Gravitational waves may have created dark matter in the early universe
items.body: In the chaotic first moments after the Big Bang, ripples in spacetime may have done more than just echo through the cosmos—they could have helped create dark matter itself. New research suggests that faint, ancient gravitational waves might have transformed into particles that eventually became the invisible substance

items.title: What counts as the woods? Judge axes Nova Scotia’s ban that defied ‘commonsense definitions’
items.body:
The court sided with a Canadian hiker who deliberately challenged the order imposed to curb spread of wildfires

As wildfires raged across Nova Scotia last summer, the Canadian province made a simple plea to residents: stay away from the woods.

As the

items.title: Private-Sector Sleuthing Becomes Big Business for US Tech Startup
items.body: Trump’s economic crackdown on China’s US interests is creating opportunities for a Utah-based intelligence firm

items.title: ‘Astonishing’ discovery could help save children from deadly disfiguring condition
items.body:
A previously unknown species of bacteria found in patients with noma could be key to creating treatments for the neglected tropical disease

The “astonishing” discovery of a new bacterium could open the door to better ways to prevent, detect and treat a fatal and disfiguring childhood disease, researchers hope.

items.title: Iran's foreign minister leaves Pakistan, then Trump cancels U.S. delegation's travel
items.body: After arriving on Friday, Iran's Abbas Araghchi has left Islamabad, prompting President Trump to announce that his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner will no longer travel there Saturday for peace talks

items.title: Altman apologizes after OpenAI failed to alert police before fatal Canada shooting
items.body:
OpenAI said the company had identified an account using abuse-detection efforts, but determined at the time it didn’t meet threshold for legal referral

The head of OpenAI has written a letter apologizing that his company didn’t alert law enforcement about the online behavior of a person who shot and killed eig

items.title: US military kills two more people in strike on alleged drug boat in Pacific
items.body:
Small boat destroyed in video posted on social media as US campaign has killed at least 178 people since September

The US military announced on Friday that it killed two people in an attack on a boat in the eastern Pacific, part of a series of dea

items.title: Vessels seized by the U.S. this week were part of a global 'shadow fleet'
items.body: NPR's Juana Summers speaks with Elisabeth Braw, senior fellow at the Atlantic Council, about shadow vessels, after the U.S. military's seizure of two Iranian-linked oil tankers.
